elements.sent-2411-001: Glazunov \end{center \begin{center {\rm Institute of Mathematics and Informatics Bulgarian Academy of Sciences {\rm Glushkov Institute of Cybernetics NASU, Kiev, {\rm Email: {\it glanm@yahoo.com \end{center {\bf Keywords: lattice packing, Minkowski ball, Minkowski domain, critical lattice, optimal lattice packing, lattice covering.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-002: We construct and study extremal functions, which are defined by distance functions of convex bodies.
elements.sent-2411-003: Such functions take values in the moduli spaces of geometric objects associated with these convex bodies.
elements.sent-2411-004: Examples of such functions are the homogeneous arithmetic minimum of a function in a lattice, the Hermite constant, and the critical determinant of a body.
elements.sent-2411-005: We define and investigate extremal functions that yield optimal packings of bodies, the best values of covering constants, and optimal solutions to Diophantine approximation problems.
elements.sent-2411-006: Moreover, for two-dimensional unit Minkowski balls and Minkowski domains we determine the minimal areas of inscribed and circumscribed hexagons.
elements.sent-2411-007: Extremal problems and functions arise in the study of many mathematical problems: properties of the circle and the sphere and isoperimetric inequalities; Chebyshev polynomials as polynomials of least deviation from zero; homogeneous arithmetic minimum of a distance function in a lattice; finding the Hermite constant; Minkowski's hypothesis on the critical determinant of a domain; Bernstein's inequality; Fourier analysis; the large sieve; and in others.
elements.sent-2411-008: In this communication we study extremal functions, which are defined by distance functions of convex bodies.
elements.sent-2411-009: We construct such functions as real functions that take values in the moduli spaces of geometric objects associated with these convex bodies.
elements.sent-2411-010: We define and investigate extremal functions that yield optimal packings of bodies, the best known values of covering constants solutions to Diophantine approximation problems.
elements.sent-2411-011: Let $D $ be a set and $\Lambda $ be a lattice with base \begin{equation}\{{\mathbf a}_1, \ldots ,{\mathbf a}_n \}\end{equation} in ${\mathbb R ^n.$ A lattice $\Lambda $ is {\it admissible for body $D $ ($ D$-{\it admissible ) if ${D \bigcap \Lambda = \emptyset $ or $0.$ Let \begin{equation}d(\Lambda) = |\det ({\mathbf a}_1, \ldots ,{\mathbf a}_n )|\end{equation} be the determinant of $\Lambda.$ The infimum $\Delta(D) $ ($ inf \; \Delta(D)$) of determinants of all lattices admissible for $D $ is called {\em the critical determinant of $D; $ if there is no $D-$admissible lattices then puts $\Delta(D) = \infty.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-012: We investigate convex symmetric distance functions: (4) $F({\mathbf x +{\mathbf y ) \le F({\mathbf x ) + F({\mathbf y )$, (5) $F(-{\mathbf x ) = F({\mathbf x )$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: In the cases we are considering for a natural number $n$, such distance functions have the form \begin{equation}\label{df} F({\mathbf x}) = ( |x_1|^p + \cdots + |x_n|^p )^\frac{1}{p}\end{equation} To the convex symmetric distance function $F({\mathbf x )$ corresponds fixed bounded symmetric about origin convex body ({\it centrally symmetric convex body) \begin{equation}\label{sb} F({\mathbf x}) < 1.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-014: For real $p > 1$ equation (\ref{sb ) defines {\it strictly convex bodies that do not contain {\it straight line segments on the boundary .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: We investigate {\it strictly convex centrally symmetric bodies , which correspond to the parameter $p > 1$, as well as the limiting cases $p=1$ and $p=\infty$ (for two-dimensional bodies of the form (\ref{sb ), the cases $p = 1$ and $p=\infty$ were already investigated by Minkowski).}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Let $x_1, \cdots x_n \in {\mathbb R}$ and let \begin{equation}\label{smt} D_p: \; |x_1|^p + \cdots + |x_n|^p < 1, \; p \ge 1\end{equation} be $n$-dimensional (open) Minkowski's balls with boundary \begin{equation}\label{mt} {{\mathbb S}^{n-1}_c}: \; |x_1|^c + \cdots + |x_n|^c = 1, \; c \ge 1.\end{equation}. (Minkowski's spheres).
elements.sent-2411-017: Let $D$ be a fixed bounded symmetric about origin convex body ({\it centrally symmetric convex body for short) with volume $V(D)$.}
elements.sent-2411-018: If $D$ is symmetric about the origin and convex, then $2D$ is convex and symmetric about the origin.
elements.sent-2411-019: Let $m$ be integer $m \ge 0$ and $n$ be natural greater $m$.
elements.sent-2411-020: If $2^m D$ centrally symmetric convex body then $2^n D$ is again centrally symmetrc convex body.

elements.sent-2411-022: They have the next forms: The Minkowski-Cohn moduli space of admissible lattices of Minkowski balls has the form \begin{equation}\label{mcms} \Delta(p,\sigma) = (\tau + \sigma)(1 + \tau^{p})^{-\frac{1}{p}} (1 + \sigma^p)^{-\frac{1}{p}},\end{equation} in the domain \begin{equation}\label{msd} {\mathcal M}: \; \infty > p > 1, \; 1 \leq \sigma \leq \sigma_{p} = (2^p - 1)^{\frac{1}{p}},\end{equation} of the $ \{p,\sigma\} $-plane, where $\sigma$ is some real parameter Hence the Minkowski-Cohn moduli space for admissible lattices of doubled Minkowski balls $2 D_p$ (Minkowski domains $2 D_p, \; m = 1$) has the form \begin{equation}\Delta(p,\sigma)_{2 D_p} = 4 (\tau + \sigma)(1 + \tau^{p})^{-\frac{1}{p}} (1 + \sigma^p)^{-\frac{1}{p}},\end{equation} in the same domain (\ref{msd ).}

elements.sent-2411-039: Distance functions (see section \ref{nd ) can themselves be extremal functions.}
elements.sent-2411-040: For example, in the well-known isoperimetric problem about a circle, which is still Archimedes began to study, the function $(x^2 + y^2)^\frac{1 {2}$ itself is an extremal function, bounding the convex region $x^2 + y^2 < 1$ of the area $A = \pi$ of the curve of length $L = 2\pi$, which turns the isoperimetric inequality $4\pi A \le L^2$ into equality.}
elements.sent-2411-041: For a sphere, the isoperimetric problem is more complicated, but the distance function$(x^2 + y^2 + z^2)^\frac{1 {2}$ also plays a key role.}

elements.sent-2411-043: We define and investigate extremal functions that give optimal solutions to Diophantine approximation problems, optimal packings of Minkowski balls and Minkowski domains, and the best current values of covering constants.

elements.sent-2411-074: Our object of study is extremal functions which are defined by distance functions of convex bodies.
elements.sent-2411-075: These functions take values in the moduli spaces of algebraic and geometric objects associated with these ${\mathbb Z}$-modules (geometric lattices) and with convex bodies.
elements.sent-2411-076: In most cases, convex bodies are $2$-dimensional Minkowski balls whose boundaries are Minkowski curves and we study lattice points on these curves.
elements.sent-2411-077: We define and investigate extremal functions that yield the homogeneous arithmetic minimum of a function in a lattice, the Hermite constant, the critical determinant of a body, optimal packings of bodies, best values of covering constants, and optimal solutions of Diophantine approximation problems.
elements.sent-2411-078: One of the algebraic foundations of the Geometry of Numbers are ${\mathbb Z}$-modules (geometric lattices), which are associated with the geometric bodies studied in the problems.
elements.sent-2411-079: For a more complete study of these objects, the metric properties of the space ${\mathbb R ^n$ are used (Minkowski's convex body theorem, and others).}
elements.sent-2411-080: In Section \ref{mtanf we briefly discuss the connection of our research with Minkowski's theory for algebraic number fields.}
elements.sent-2411-081: The existence of extremal functions within the framework of the study is based on the fact that in formulas (\ref{cd ), (\ref{ham ), (\ref{ha ) the infimum and supremum are achieved.}}}

elements.sent-2411-102: For $1 < p < \infty$ Minkowski curves are strictly convex curves.
elements.sent-2411-103: The length $\ell$ of the Minkowski curve for $p > 1$ is \begin{equation}{\ell} = 4 \int_0^1 (1 - y^p)^{\frac{1}{p}} dy\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-104: As $\ell > 3$ by results of the Jarnic \cite{jar the curves $C_p$ and for integer $N > 0$ their $N$-fold magnifications $N\cdot C_p$ about the origin for corresponding length $\ell $ contains at most \begin{equation}3(4\pi)^{-\frac{1}{3}} {\ell}^{\frac{2}{3}} + {\mathcal O}({\ell}^{\frac{1}{3}})\end{equation} integral lattice points.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The curve $C_p$ for real $p > 1$ is three times continuity differentiable.
elements.sent-2411-106: From results of Swinnerton–Dyer \cite{sdy follow that the number of rational points $(\frac{m {N ,\frac{n {N )$ on $C_p$ (the number of integral points on $N\cdot C_p$) as $N \to \infty$ is estimatrd by \begin{equation}|N \cdot C_p \cap {\mathbb Z}^2| \le c(C_p, \epsilon)N^{\frac{3}{5} + \epsilon}.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-107: Let $y = f(x)$ be the arc $A$ of $C_{p}$ and $f(x)$ be a transcendental analytic function on $\left[0, 1\right]$.. Then \begin{equation}|tA \cap {\mathbb Z}^2| \le c(f,\epsilon) t^{\epsilon}.\end{equation}.

elements.sent-2411-110: The function field ${\mathbb F}$ on the Riemann surface is the field of rational functions of the genus $g$.
elements.sent-2411-111: If two such fields are isomorphic then the corresponding algebraic curves are birationaly isomorphic.

elements.sent-2411-117: For relatively compact open set $U \subset {\mathcal A}$ and for holomorphic map $\varphi: \mathcal A \to C_{2 d ({\mathbb C )$ with Zariski dense set $\varphi(\mathcal A)$ and the set $S_U(T) = \{ z \in U | \varphi(z) \in C_{2 d (K)\}$ and $h_{\mathcal L (\varphi(z)) \le T$ for cardinality $\#S_U(T)$of the set $S_U(T)$ and for any positive number $\epsilon$ we have \begin{equation}\#S_U(T) \ll \exp (\epsilon T)\end{equation} where the involved constants depend on $U, \varphi$, and ${\mathcal L}$ but not on $T$ .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-118: The set of areas of the entire family of ( $ {\mathcal{H _p, (1 < p < \infty)$ circumscribed to unit Minkowski balls has the form: \begin{equation}{\mathbf {AC}}(p,\sigma)_{2 D_p} = 4 (\tau + \sigma)(1 + \tau^{p})^{-\frac{1}{p}} (1 + \sigma^p)^{-\frac{1}{p}},\end{equation} in the same domain (\ref{msd ).}}}
